So the program has both a long main (0x401740) and a short main (0x4018cf). But only one can be the entry point. The entry point is actually _start at 0x401ea0 which calls __libc_start_main with main at 0x401740.

Wait, I need to check: what does the program ACTUALLY DO when run? It creates an image with a ray-traced scene (sphere, ground, sky). The code at 0x401740 is the main function that does this, calling vector_normalize, sphere_intersect, etc.

But I also found a "sky_color" at 0x401865 which seems to be part of the real main at 0x4018cf. Let me check if 0x4018cf is actually the main function or if 0x401740 is the real main.

Looking at the disassembly:
- 0x401740 matches the behavior (calls allocate_image, writes progress, calls sphere_intersect, writes image)
- 0x4018cf is much simpler

Both might exist. Let me check which one is the actual entry point by looking at _start which is at 0x401ea0. The _start function calls __libc_start_main with main at 0x401740.

So the real main IS at 0x401740. The code at 0x4018cf is a separate function (maybe an older version or a different utility).

Given the complexity of reverse-engineering the complete ray tracer, let me focus on writing a C program that can produce the same output by implementing a simple ray tracer that matches the visual output.

So I need to write a C program that, when compiled and run, produces exactly the same output as the pre-compiled /app/mystery.
